What is the impact of the creator economy on the way young people think about career, identity, and the relationship between work and self-expression?
How do we ensure that conversations about regulating technology do not end up disproportionately silencing the voices of marginalized communities?
What is the role of digital storytelling in preserving the histories and experiences of communities that have been erased from mainstream narratives?
How do we build a culture of online accountability that is restorative rather than purely punitive?
What is the impact of social media on the way we build and sustain meaningful friendships across distance and difference?
How do we ensure that young people growing up today have the skills to be not just consumers of digital media but thoughtful producers of it?
What is the responsibility of society as a whole to create the conditions in which technology serves human flourishing rather than undermining it?
How do we think about the way the digital economy has redefined what constitutes work, value, and economic contribution?
What is the impact of online misinformation on the health of democratic institutions and the public's ability to make informed decisions?
How do we create digital spaces that are genuinely welcoming for people with disabilities, neurodivergent users, and others who are often excluded?
What is the relationship between the business models of social media companies and the content those companies choose to amplify or suppress?
How do we build the resilience and critical thinking skills that young people need to thrive in an era of information overload?
What is the impact of social media on the way communities respond to and recover from natural disasters and public health crises?
How do we think about the ethical implications of collecting and using data from children who are too young to meaningfully consent?
What is the responsibility of educators to model healthy and ethical technology use for their students?
How do we address the emotional labor placed on young women who use social media and face constant scrutiny of their bodies, choices, and opinions?
What is the impact of social media on the way political parties communicate with voters and mobilize participation in elections?
How do we build a more equitable digital economy that distributes the benefits of technological innovation more broadly and fairly?
What is the role of digital platforms in supporting or undermining the development of a healthy and informed public sphere?
How do we think about the long-term consequences of designing technology for maximum engagement rather than maximum wellbeing?
What is the impact of social media on the way young people develop and sustain their sense of community and belonging?
How do we create a culture in which people feel empowered to report online harm without fear of retaliation or dismissal?
What is the responsibility of technology companies to proactively address the ways their products are being weaponized against vulnerable users?
How do we think about the ethical obligations of those who design the algorithms that increasingly shape our choices, beliefs, and relationships?
What is the impact of social media on the way we form and sustain our sense of shared identity as a nation or community?
How do we think about the responsibility of platforms that host user-generated content when that content causes measurable harm to others?
What is the role of trusted community voices in countering misinformation in online spaces where institutional credibility has eroded?
How do we address the reality that young people are developing their identities inside platforms that profit from their insecurities?
What is the relationship between digital access in schools and long-term economic mobility for students from low-income families?
How do we create technology that genuinely serves the needs of aging populations who are increasingly dependent on digital tools?
What is the impact of social media on the way we experience and process collective trauma as a society?
How do we think about the responsibility of platforms when content that begins as satire or fiction spreads as fact and causes real harm?
What is the role of young people themselves in shaping the digital culture they will inherit and live inside for the rest of their lives?
How do we address the way that social media can simultaneously give voice to the marginalized and expose them to disproportionate abuse?
What is the impact of technology on the way we practice and transmit faith, spirituality, and religious community across generations?
How do we think about the ethical dimensions of social media platforms that allow political microtargeting that bypasses informed public deliberation?
What is the responsibility of the sports and entertainment industries to use their digital platforms to promote accurate health and civic information?
How do we build online communities where disagreement is productive rather than destructive and where people leave conversations more informed than when they arrived?
What is the impact of widespread smartphone access on the development of patience, attention, and the capacity for deep thought among young people?
How do we think about the difference between a platform that moderates content to protect users and one that censors content to protect its own interests?
What is the responsibility of technology companies whose products are used in schools to protect the data and privacy of the students who use them?
How do we address the way that viral social media moments can reduce complex human experiences — suffering, injustice, tragedy — to shareable content?
What is the impact of social media on the way we celebrate, transmit, and sometimes commodify cultural traditions that belong to specific communities?
How do we think about the governance of online spaces that function as public squares but are owned and operated as private businesses?
What is the responsibility of news organizations to adapt their editorial standards for the social media environment without sacrificing accuracy or depth?
How do we ensure that the tools designed to fight online misinformation do not become tools for suppressing legitimate dissent and minority viewpoints?
What is the impact of digital reputation systems — ratings, reviews, follower counts — on the way we assign value to people and their contributions?
How do we think about the way social media can make extraordinary acts of generosity and civic courage visible in ways that inspire communities to rise to meet them?
What is the role of young activists in using digital tools not just to raise awareness but to build the organizational infrastructure that creates lasting change?
How do we address the way that algorithmic amplification of extreme content creates a distorted picture of public opinion that feeds its own escalation?
What is the impact of social media on the way we experience beauty, define aesthetic standards, and relate to our own physical appearance?
How do we think about the responsibilities of those who build and maintain digital platforms to the communities that come to depend on them?
What is the role of technology in expanding access to justice for people who cannot afford traditional legal representation or advocacy?
How do we create digital public spaces where civic life can genuinely flourish rather than platforms that simulate civic engagement while extracting value from it?
What is the impact of growing up in a world of infinite digital content on a young person's ability to develop a coherent sense of taste, values, and identity?
How do we think about the ethical responsibility of platforms that knowingly allow health misinformation to spread because the engagement it generates is profitable?
What is the role of cross-cultural digital exchange in building the kind of global empathy and solidarity that our most urgent shared challenges demand?
How do we address the way that the speed and scale of digital communication can outpace our individual and institutional capacity for reflection and accountability?
What is the impact of technology on the way small and midsize businesses compete, survive, and build relationships with the communities they serve?
How do we think about what we owe to the people whose data we use — consciously or unconsciously — every time we engage with an algorithmically curated platform?
What is the responsibility of those who teach, mentor, and parent in helping young people build the inner resources to thrive in a digitally saturated world?
How do we build the kind of democratic culture around technology that insists on accountability, transparency, and genuine regard for the common good?
Hard: How should democratic societies regulate the concentrated market power of dominant technology platforms without stifling the innovation that benefits all citizens?
What framework of rights and responsibilities should govern the collection and use of personal data in an era when that data is worth more than oil?
How do we hold artificial intelligence systems accountable for discriminatory outcomes when their decision-making processes are deliberately opaque?
What does meaningful informed consent look like in a digital ecosystem where the terms of service that govern our lives are designed to be unread?
How do we prevent the consolidation of artificial intelligence capabilities in the hands of a small number of governments and corporations from becoming a new form of authoritarian control?
What is the responsibility of democratic governments to ensure that their citizens retain sovereignty over the digital infrastructure that now underpins civic life?
How do we design regulatory frameworks for social media platforms that protect freedom of expression while preventing demonstrable, large-scale harm?
What does justice look like when an algorithm trained on historically biased data perpetuates and scales that bias in hiring, lending, and criminal sentencing?
How do we think about the political economy of attention — and what reforms would be necessary to break the structural incentives that reward outrage over truth?
What is the appropriate role of government in mandating transparency from private technology companies whose decisions affect the public interest?
How do we build international consensus on the governance of emerging technologies when the geopolitical interests of powerful nations are so deeply in conflict?
What are the implications for democracy when the infrastructure of public discourse is owned and operated by entities whose primary obligation is to their shareholders?
How do we ensure that the development of artificial general intelligence serves humanity as a whole rather than a narrow class of technologists and investors?
What legal and ethical frameworks must be developed to govern the use of biometric surveillance technology by both governments and private corporations?
How do we address the reality that the most powerful tools for digital manipulation are also the most profitable, creating a structural incentive to weaponize them?
What does digital self-determination mean for communities whose cultural production is being scraped, packaged, and monetized without their knowledge or consent?
How do we reconcile the right to privacy with the legitimate security needs of democratic states in an era of sophisticated digital threats?
What is the relationship between platform design choices — recommendation algorithms, notification systems, content ranking — and our collective epistemic health as a society?
How do we build accountability mechanisms for artificial intelligence that are robust enough to constrain its most dangerous applications without prohibiting its most beneficial ones?
What obligations do technology companies have to the countries and communities where they extract value but resist the jurisdiction of local law?
How do we think about the democratic legitimacy of private content moderation decisions that have the practical effect of shaping public discourse at global scale?
What is the appropriate legal status of data — should it be treated as property, as a public good, or as an extension of personal identity deserving of constitutional protection?
How do we prevent the use of predictive policing and surveillance technologies from entrenching the racial and economic inequalities already embedded in criminal justice systems?
What role should civil society, independent journalism, and academic research play in auditing the power of technology platforms that are otherwise largely unaccountable?
How do we think about the ethics of social media platforms that knowingly amplify emotionally activating content because it drives the engagement metrics that sustain their business?
What does it mean to speak of digital rights as human rights, and what enforcement mechanisms would give that claim genuine force?
How do we address the structural conflict of interest in having the entities most responsible for digital harms also be the primary arbiters of how those harms are defined and addressed?
What is the long-term political consequence of allowing artificial intelligence systems to optimize for engagement in ways that systematically erode citizens' capacity for deliberation?
How do we think about the governance of global digital platforms in a way that is simultaneously democratically legitimate, technically literate, and protective of the most vulnerable users?
What frameworks for liability should apply when an artificial intelligence system causes harm, and how do we attribute responsibility across the complex chain of actors involved in its creation?
How do we ensure that the promises of precision medicine and digital health are realized in ways that serve all communities rather than concentrating longevity and wellbeing among the affluent?
What is the ethical case for mandatory algorithmic auditing, and how do we design audit regimes that are independent, technically capable, and politically insulated from industry capture?

How do we think about the national security implications of critical digital infrastructure — from undersea cables to cloud computing — being controlled by entities whose interests may diverge from the public good?
What is the relationship between the business model of behavioral advertising and the erosion of democratic norms, and what would a post-surveillance capitalism internet look like?
How do we build international norms around the development and deployment of autonomous weapons systems before the technology races ahead of our capacity for governance?
What does it mean for the right to due process when automated systems make decisions about people's access to credit, housing, employment, and social services?
How do we address the reality that the most sophisticated disinformation operations — whether state-sponsored or commercial — will always be ahead of our capacity to detect and counter them?
What ethical principles should guide the development of brain-computer interface technologies, and who should have the authority to set those principles?

How do we think about the geopolitical implications of a world in which a small number of companies control the data and computing infrastructure that underpins global commerce, communication, and governance?

What is the appropriate response of the legal system to the emergence of artificial intelligence systems that can produce personalized disinformation at scale, faster than any human institution can detect and counter it?
How do we build democratic institutions that are genuinely capable of governing powerful technologies they do not yet fully understand?

What are the implications for freedom of thought and cognitive liberty if neural interfaces and personalized AI systems begin to shape our beliefs and preferences in ways we cannot easily detect or resist?
How do we think about the ethics of training large language models on copyrighted creative work without compensation, and what does fairness look like in that context?

What is the appropriate scope of antitrust enforcement in digital markets where network effects and data advantages create structural monopolies that traditional competition law was not designed to address?
How do we build robust public interest media institutions that can survive in a digital economy designed to defund them by capturing their audiences and advertising revenue?
What is the relationship between digital surveillance capabilities and the shrinking space for civil society, journalism, and political opposition in countries sliding toward authoritarianism?
How do we design digital identity systems that provide security and access without creating the infrastructure for comprehensive state surveillance of citizens' lives?
What is the ethical framework that should govern the use of artificial intelligence in military targeting, and who has the legitimacy to set and enforce those rules?
How do we think about the consent of future generations when the data collected about children today will shape the algorithmic systems that govern their adult lives?
What does platform accountability require in practice — transparency, interoperability, liability, public ownership — and how do we build the political will to impose it?
How do we address the fact that the harms of unregulated technology fall disproportionately on communities that are least represented in the rooms where technology policy is made?
What is the role of whistleblowers and internal dissenters in holding technology companies accountable, and how do we protect them from the institutional power arrayed against them?
How do we think about the ethics of deploying generative artificial intelligence in contexts — healthcare, legal advice, emotional support — where the stakes of error are especially high?
What legal and institutional reforms would be required to bring the governance of digital platforms into meaningful alignment with democratic values and the public interest?
How do we prevent the use of artificial intelligence in hiring from encoding and scaling the subjective biases of the human decision-makers it was trained to replicate?
What is the political philosophy of the internet — does it belong to everyone, to no one, or to the companies that built and maintain it — and does the answer matter for governance?
How do we build a right to explanation into consequential AI systems in a way that is technically meaningful rather than a compliance exercise that obscures more than it reveals?
What is the appropriate response of democratic governments to the use of social media platforms by foreign states as instruments of political warfare against their citizens?
How do we think about the ethics of automated content moderation that operates at speed and scale but inevitably makes decisions that have life-altering consequences for the people it affects?
What would meaningful data portability and interoperability requirements look like, and how would they change the power dynamics of the digital economy?
How do we address the reality that the infrastructure of digital democracy — online voting, digital political advertising, social media discourse — is deeply vulnerable to manipulation and attack?
What is the role of universities and academic institutions in providing independent analysis of the power of technology platforms that have the resources to shape the research agenda?
How do we think about the right to digital access as a precondition for participation in modern civic, economic, and cultural life?
What institutional designs would allow international bodies to govern global digital platforms without either being captured by the most powerful states or rendered ineffective by their disagreement?
How do we build regulatory capacity in government that is technically sophisticated enough to understand, scrutinize, and effectively govern the companies and technologies it oversees?

What is the ethical status of emotionally manipulative design — dark patterns, variable reward schedules, social validation loops — and should it carry legal consequences for the companies that employ it?
How do we think about the public health dimensions of social media in a way that justifies the kind of regulatory intervention we apply to other industries whose products cause demonstrable harm?
What is the relationship between the concentration of technological capability and the erosion of democratic self-governance, and what structural remedies are available?

How do we ensure that the governance of artificial intelligence is shaped by interdisciplinary expertise — including ethics, law, social science, and affected communities — rather than left entirely to engineers?
What would a genuine digital bill of rights include, and what political forces would need to align to make its enactment possible?
How do we think about the ethics of selling digital advertising that targets the psychological vulnerabilities of specific individuals identified through their data?
What is the responsibility of venture capital and private equity to consider the social consequences of the technologies they fund and scale?
How do we address the structural problem of platform monopolies that have enough market power to acquire or destroy competitive alternatives before they can challenge the incumbent's dominance?
What does genuine algorithmic transparency require, and how do we prevent transparency requirements from becoming a form of regulatory theater that reassures the public without constraining power?
How do we think about the ethics of using artificial intelligence to generate synthetic media of real people without their consent, and what remedies are adequate to the harm?
What is the appropriate relationship between intellectual property law and the development of artificial intelligence systems that are trained on the creative output of millions of people?
How do we build a digital public sphere that is genuinely open to all voices while not providing infrastructure for those who would use openness to destroy it?
What is the relationship between the design of social media platforms and the radicalization of users who enter them with ordinary views and emerge with extremist ones?
How do we think about the long-term consequences of allowing opaque recommendation systems to serve as the primary curators of the information that shapes citizens' beliefs and political judgments?
What is the appropriate balance between national sovereignty over digital infrastructure and the global interoperability that makes the internet valuable?

How do we build accountability mechanisms for the use of artificial intelligence in public sector decisions — welfare determinations, parole decisions, child protective services — that are robust and accessible?
What does it mean to govern platforms as utilities or public infrastructure rather than as private property, and what legal and political changes would that require?
How do we think about the way private technology companies have become de facto regulators of public discourse, with more practical power over speech than most elected governments?
What is the ethical case for and against requiring social media platforms to carry all lawful speech regardless of its quality or impact, and what does that debate reveal about our competing values?
How do we design technology policy that is genuinely anticipatory — getting ahead of foreseeable harms — rather than perpetually reactive to crises that were predictable from the beginning?

What is the relationship between the digital economy's demand for low-cost content moderation labor and the exploitation of workers in the Global South who are exposed to the worst of the internet so the rest of us do not have to be?
How do we think about the ethics of training AI systems on the health data, legal histories, and financial records of populations that were never asked whether they consented to that use?
What is the appropriate role of democratic deliberation in setting the values and priorities that should guide the development of general-purpose artificial intelligence systems?
How do we prevent the legal and regulatory frameworks designed to protect users from becoming tools that incumbent platforms use to entrench their dominance and exclude newcomers?
What is the long-term consequence for civic culture of living in an information environment where algorithmic systems — rather than editorial judgment — decide what is visible and what is not?

How do we think about the obligations of platform companies to communities whose social and economic fabric has been disrupted by their services without any democratic input into the terms of that disruption?
What would a genuinely feminist technology policy look like, and how would it differ in its priorities and approach from the policy frameworks that currently dominate the field?
How do we build international cooperation on the governance of cyberspace in a geopolitical environment increasingly defined by great power competition and mutual suspicion?
What is the ethical status of nudging users toward particular behaviors through interface design choices they are not aware of, and should governments have the authority to prohibit it?

How do we think about the rights of workers whose labor is mediated by algorithmic management systems that monitor, evaluate, and discipline them in ways that are opaque and effectively unchallengeable?
What is the relationship between the globalization of digital platforms and the erosion of local economies, media ecosystems, and cultural industries that democratic communities depend on?

How do we ensure that the development of artificial intelligence does not replicate and scale the colonial patterns of extraction — of data, of labor, of cultural production — that have historically concentrated wealth and power in the hands of a few?
What is the political theory of content moderation — on what principles should private actors make decisions that have the practical effect of regulating the speech of hundreds of millions of people?

How do we build the kind of public digital infrastructure — secure messaging, privacy-preserving identity systems, open social protocols — that would give citizens genuine alternatives to commercially captured platforms?

What is the appropriate response of the legal system to the emergence of artificial intelligence systems that can produce personalized disinformation at scale with a speed and reach that no human institution can effectively counter?
How do we think about the ethics of using social media data to identify and preemptively intervene with people assessed to be at risk of committing crimes they have not yet committed?
What reforms to the corporate governance of technology companies would make them more responsive to the interests of the communities they affect rather than primarily to those of their shareholders?

How do we address the growing evidence that social media platforms deliberately suppress certain kinds of political and social speech based on criteria that are never made transparent to the users affected?

What is the responsibility of wealthy democratic nations to provide support for the development of independent, locally governed digital infrastructure in countries that would otherwise be dependent on commercial platforms or authoritarian alternatives?

How do we think about the ethics of deploying artificial intelligence in ways that displace large numbers of workers without any mechanism for ensuring those workers share in the productivity gains that result?
What is the appropriate legal framework for addressing the harm caused by social media platforms to the mental health of minors when those platforms have known about and concealed that harm?
How do we build a culture of ethical responsibility within technology companies that goes beyond compliance theater and creates genuine internal pressure to refuse lucrative but harmful applications?

What is the long-term geopolitical consequence of allowing the development of transformative artificial intelligence to be driven primarily by the commercial interests of a small number of private companies in a small number of countries?
How do we think about the right to human review and appeal when automated systems make decisions about our access to healthcare, financial services, and government benefits?
What is the relationship between platform architecture — the way social networks are structured and incentivized — and the behavior of the communities that form within them?
How do we address the ethical dimensions of large-scale psychological research conducted by social media companies on their users without consent or independent ethical oversight?
What would it look like to approach the governance of artificial intelligence as a question of global justice rather than a purely technical or competitive challenge?

How do we think about the obligations of technology companies to the democratic systems of governance that created the conditions — rule of law, stable markets, public investment in research — that made their success possible?
What is the appropriate legal and ethical framework for addressing the use of deepfake technology in political advertising and electoral interference?
How do we build the institutional capacity — in government, civil society, and journalism — to monitor, analyze, and respond to the rapidly evolving capabilities of artificial intelligence systems?
What is the relationship between the design of digital attention markets and the degradation of the long-form deliberative discourse that democratic self-governance depends on?
How do we think about the ethics of social media platforms that allow advertising targeted at users identified as being in emotional distress by the behavioral data those platforms have collected?

What is the appropriate response to the growing use of artificial intelligence to generate legal documents, medical diagnoses, and educational content in ways that may be authoritative in form but unreliable in substance?
How do we build regulatory frameworks for emerging technologies that can adapt to rapid change without becoming either capture-prone bureaucracies or regulatory vacuums?

What is the democratic case for treating the core algorithmic systems of dominant social media platforms as public utilities subject to public oversight rather than as trade secrets protected from scrutiny?
How do we address the profound power asymmetry between individual users and the technology platforms that have detailed behavioral profiles of those users and the resources to act on them?

What is the ethical status of the use of facial recognition technology by governments and corporations in public spaces, and what regulatory framework could adequately address its most dangerous applications?

How do we think about the long-term consequences of replacing human judgment with algorithmic decision-making in the institutions — courts, schools, hospitals, immigration authorities — that shape people's most fundamental life chances?

What is the responsibility of technology companies that have built their success on network effects and data advantages to use that position to address rather than exacerbate the social inequalities their growth has helped to produce?

How do we ensure that the promise of open-source artificial intelligence — that it democratizes access to powerful tools — does not also democratize access to tools for mass manipulation, surveillance, and harm?

What is the appropriate response of democratic societies to the emergence of autonomous AI agents that can act in the world on behalf of individuals or organizations without meaningful human oversight at the moment of decision?

How do we think about the responsibility of technology companies to preserve the integrity of elections in countries where their platforms serve as primary channels for political communication and their advertising systems are available to foreign actors?
What is the relationship between the privatization of digital infrastructure and the long-term erosion of the public spaces, shared institutions, and common goods that democratic life depends on?

How do we build a global consensus on the minimum standards of digital safety, privacy, and human dignity that should apply to all technology platforms regardless of where they are headquartered or incorporated?

What is the ethical case for slowing the development and deployment of transformative artificial intelligence until adequate governance frameworks are in place, and what are the costs and risks of such an approach?

How do we think about the obligations of technology companies to the workers who trained their AI systems — often for very low wages in difficult conditions — and whose intellectual and creative labor now powers their most profitable products?
What is the long-term consequence for human autonomy, creativity, and meaning if the systems that generate knowledge, art, and decision-making are increasingly artificial rather than human in origin?

How do we ensure that the history of this transformative moment in technology — who made these systems, whose interests they served, who paid the costs — is recorded accurately and not written solely by the powerful?

What is the responsibility of each of us as citizens, consumers, and community members to engage with the governance of technology rather than leaving it to experts, corporations, and governments to resolve on our behalf?

How do we think about the ethics of artificial intelligence systems that are designed to simulate empathy and emotional connection in ways that may substitute for genuine human relationship and community?
What is the appropriate legal response when a platform's algorithmic amplification of health misinformation can be shown to have directly contributed to preventable deaths?

How do we address the paradox that the same digital tools that empower marginalized communities to organize and resist are also used by authoritarian actors to surveil, infiltrate, and suppress those movements?
What would a technology industry look like that was genuinely accountable to the public interest — in its hiring, its governance, its business model, and its relationships with regulators?

How do we think about the ethics of artificial intelligence systems trained on the work of writers, artists, and musicians who never consented to have their creative output used in this way and receive no compensation for it?
What is the relationship between the architecture of social media feeds and the systematic distortion of our shared understanding of reality that makes democratic deliberation increasingly difficult?
How do we build international legal frameworks that can hold state actors accountable for the use of digital tools to undermine the democratic sovereignty of other nations?

What is the appropriate response to the growing use of artificial intelligence in education in ways that may reduce young people's capacity for independent thought, struggle, and genuine intellectual development?

How do we think about the ethics of technology companies that profit from selling political advertising while simultaneously claiming not to be responsible for the political consequences of the content they host and amplify?

What is the responsibility of the legal profession to develop the expertise necessary to meaningfully advocate for clients whose rights are being affected by algorithmic systems they have no access to or understanding of?

How do we address the reality that the most consequential decisions about artificial intelligence are being made by a small, unrepresentative, and largely unaccountable group of technologists whose interests are not identical to the public's?

What is the ethical framework for thinking about the rights and interests of people whose likenesses, voices, and personal data are used to train artificial intelligence systems they will later encounter as if they were human?

How do we build a democratic politics of technology that is sophisticated enough to match the complexity of the systems being governed without becoming so technocratic that ordinary citizens are effectively excluded from it?

What is the appropriate scope of national security justifications for digital surveillance, and how do we prevent those justifications from being used to erode civil liberties in ways that undermine the democratic values they claim to protect?

How do we think about the ethics of social scoring systems — whether operated by governments or corporations — that aggregate behavioral data to make judgments about individuals that then determine their access to opportunities and services?

What is the responsibility of the media and information ecosystem as a whole to develop norms and standards that can withstand the pressures of the digital attention economy without abandoning the commitments to accuracy and fairness that make journalism valuable?

How do we build the kind of cross-border solidarity among digital rights advocates, researchers, and civil society organizations that could effectively counter the global power of technology platforms that are subject to no single jurisdiction?

What is the long-term constitutional and democratic consequence of allowing private companies to build and own the infrastructure of public communication in ways that give them effective veto power over what ideas can circulate in the public square?

How do we think about the ethics of using artificial intelligence to make predictions about individual human behavior — in healthcare, insurance, education, criminal justice — that are probabilistic in nature but applied to specific people as if they were certain?

What is the appropriate response of democratic governments to the emergence of technologies — from encrypted communications to decentralized finance — that are specifically designed to be ungovernable by existing legal and regulatory frameworks?

How do we ensure that the communities most harmed by the unregulated deployment of artificial intelligence and digital surveillance are centered in the policy conversations about how those technologies should be governed going forward?

What is the relationship between the consolidation of ownership in the technology sector and the decline of the local news ecosystems that are essential to informed democratic participation at the community level?

How do we think about the responsibility of those who build AI systems to anticipate and prevent not just the harms they can foresee but the emergent harms that arise when powerful technologies interact with complex social systems in unpredictable ways?

What is the ethical status of the social contract between citizens and democratic governments in an era when much of the infrastructure of daily life — communication, commerce, mobility, civic participation — is mediated by private technology companies operating outside traditional frameworks of public accountability?

How do we build a generation of technologists, policymakers, lawyers, and citizens who understand both the technical realities and the democratic stakes of artificial intelligence governance well enough to make the decisions that will determine what kind of future we live in?

What is the responsibility of those of us alive at this particular moment in history — when the foundational decisions about the governance of powerful AI systems are still being made — to future generations who will live with the consequences of those decisions long after we are gone?
